title: Revelation 14
intro: Rev 14 is the **three-angels'-messages chapter** — the doctrinal heart of historicist Adventism — and also the chapter that frames the **144,000** (vv. 1-5) and the **harvest / vintage** climax (vv. 14-20). Smith and Bohr converge on the **historicist spine**: Mt Zion = the redeemed; the three angels = three successive end-time messages preached from the early 19th century onward (first angel = hour-of-judgment 1840-1844; second angel = 1844 Babylon-fallen plus future Loud-Cry repeat; third angel = mark-of-the-beast warning culminating in Sabbath/Sunday issue); v. 13 special blessing on the dead in Christ from 1844; v. 14 Son-of-man = Christ at the second advent; vv. 14-16 harvest = ingathering of the righteous; vv. 17-20 vintage / winepress = the wicked. The divergences are concentrated in **three places**: (1) the **chronological starting point** of the first-angel proclamation, where Smith locks it to **1840-1844** Millerite preaching and Bohr extends it back to **1798** (the Rev 10:11 "prophesy again" / book unsealed); (2) the **scope** of the second angel, where Smith reads v. 8 primarily as the **1844 Millerite cry** and Bohr stages it as a **two-stage** message — 1844 + future Loud Cry of Rev 18:1-5; (3) the **chronology of the winepress** (vv. 17-20), where Smith reads the trampling as **post-millennial** (the executive judgment after the 1000 years, executed at the descent of the New Jerusalem) while Bohr reads it as **pre-second-coming / Armageddon-seventh-plague** (the wicked destroyed at Christ's return, with Rev 19:11ff as the parallel). The 144,000 framework Bohr adds beyond Smith is the **four-portraits** schema (Rev 7:1-8 sealing; 14:1-5 character; 15:2-4 victory; 19:1-9 jubilatory song), with v. 1 staged as **spiritual Mt Zion** (the worldwide remnant inside the city) over against the wicked grapes outside. Sources: Bohr, _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 7-14, 66-70, 194, 221-227, 372-378; _Revelation's Seven Trumpets_, pp. 20-21, 147, 228, 246, 263, 357-358; _Revelation's Seven Seals_, pp. 282-283, 287; _Studies on Daniel 11_, on Rev 14:6, 7 and 9-11.

verses.text: And I looked, and, lo, a Lamb stood on the mount Sion, and with him an hundred forty and four thousand, having his Father's name written in their foreheads.

pioneerReading.citation: DAR 582.3-585.2 (Smith treats vv.1-5 as a single 144,000 block; specific identifications at DAR 583.1-583.5, DAR 584.1-584.4, DAR 585.1-585.2)
pioneerReading.html: Smith identifies Mt Zion as **heavenly** (the seat of the New Jerusalem above, not earthly Palestine). On the 144,000 = the sealed of Rev 7: "**That the 144,000 here seen on Mount Zion are the saints who were just before brought to view as objects of the wrath of the beast and his image, there are the very best of reasons for believing. 1. They are identical with those sealed in Revelation 7, who have already been shown to be the righteous who are alive at the second coming of Christ**" (DAR 583.1-583.2). On the Mt-Zion-above identification: "**On what Mount Zion does John see this company standing? — The Mount Zion above; for the voice of harpers, which no doubt is uttered by these very ones, is heard from heaven; the same Zion from which the Lord utters his voice when he speaks to his people in close connection with the coming of the Son of man**" (DAR 583.5). On the Father's name in the forehead: "**They have the name of the Lamb's Father in their foreheads. In chapter 7, they are said to have the seal of God in their foreheads. An important key to an understanding of the seal of God is thus furnished us; for we at once perceive that the Father regards his name as his seal. That commandment of the law which contains God's name is therefore the seal of the law. The Sabbath commandment is the only one which has this**" (DAR 584.2).

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 7 (four-portraits list), 13-14 (GC 648-649 verbatim), 115 (harvest inside / grapes outside), 162 (worldwide spiritual Mt. Zion), 194 (winepress-and-city); _Revelation's Seven Seals_, pp. 282-283 (four portraits)
bohrReading.html: Mt Zion is the worldwide spiritual gathering of the remnant. Verbatim on the inside/outside frame: "**God's people are depicted as the harvest of the earth inside the city and the wicked are portrayed as the grapes gathered outside the city (Revelation 14:20)**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 115). Verbatim on the Mt-Zion identification: "**God's spiritual Israel (the harvest) is gathered inside worldwide spiritual Mt. Zion and the wicked spiritual Babylonians (the grapes) are gathered in the worldwide spiritual winepress outside the city**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 162). Bohr stages Rev 14:1-5 as one of **four portraits** of the 144,000: "**Revelation 14:1-5: Emphasis on the character of the 144,000**" (_Revelation's Seven Seals_, p. 282; also _Close of Probation_, p. 7). On the Father's name: Bohr quotes EGW (GC 648, 649) verbatim, intermingling Rev 14:1-5 with Rev 7 and Rev 15 line by line: "_With the Lamb upon Mount Zion [Revelation 14:1], 'having the harps of God,' [Revelation 15:2] they stand, the hundred and forty and four thousand that were redeemed from among men_" (_Close of Probation_, pp. 13-14, quoting GC 648-649). **Convergent with extension**: both readings agree the 144,000 are the last-generation translated saints; Bohr adds the explicit **four-portraits literary framework** and the **spiritual-Mt-Zion-as-worldwide-gathering** reading where Smith leaves Mt Zion as the heavenly New Jerusalem locale.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent on substance; Bohr's four-portraits framework is a Rule-V harmonization (Rev 7 + 14 + 15 + 19 read together as one composite).

symbols.label: Symbols redefined
symbols.html: Mt Zion (Smith: heavenly New Jerusalem above; Bohr: worldwide spiritual gathering of remnant on earth, inside the spiritual city).

verses.text: And I heard a voice from heaven, as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of a great thunder: and I heard the voice of harpers harping with their harps:

pioneerReading.citation: DAR 584.3 (Smith's v.2-3 song treatment, inside the 144,000 word-list of vv.1-5)
pioneerReading.html: Smith treats the harpers'-voice / new-song complex inside his numbered list on vv.1-5: "**They sing a new song which no other company is able to learn. In chapter 15:3, it is called the song of Moses and the song of the Lamb. The song of Moses, as may be seen by reference to Exodus 15, was the song of his experience and deliverance. Therefore the song of the 144,000 is the song of their deliverance. No others can join in it; for no other company will have had an experience like theirs**" (DAR 584.3).

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 13-14, quoting GC 648-649 verbatim
bohrReading.html: Bohr quotes EGW (GC 648-649) verbatim, with the v.2 phrase woven in line by line: "_and there is heard, as the sound of many waters, and as the sound of a great thunder, 'the voice of harpers harping with their harps.' [Revelation 14:2]_" (_Close of Probation_, pp. 13-14, quoting GC 648-649). Bohr cross-links this to the **Rev 15:2-4 victory-song portrait** of the 144,000 (_Revelation's Seven Seals_, p. 282).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And they sung as it were a new song before the throne, and before the four beasts, and the elders: and no man could learn that song but the hundred and forty and four thousand, which were redeemed from the earth.

pioneerReading.html: Smith reads the song as the unique experience-song of the last generation: "**They sing a new song which no other company is able to learn... The song of Moses, as may be seen by reference to Exodus 15, was the song of his experience and deliverance. Therefore the song of the 144,000 is the song of their deliverance. No others can join in it; for no other company will have had an experience like theirs**" (DAR 584.3). The class is further specified at DAR 583.4: redeemed from among men = translated from among the living, i.e. the living saints at the second coming.

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 13-14, quoting GC 648-649 verbatim
bohrReading.html: "_And they sing 'a new song' before the throne, a song which no man can learn save the hundred and forty and four thousand [Revelation 14:3]. It is the song of Moses and the Lamb — a song of deliverance [Revelation 15:3]. None but the hundred and forty-four thousand can learn that song [Revelation 14:3]_" (_Close of Probation_, pp. 13-14, quoting GC 648-649). Bohr links this to the **third 144,000-portrait** (Rev 15:2-4): "**Revelation 15:2-4: Emphasis on the victory of the 144,000 over the beast, his image and his mark**" (_Revelation's Seven Seals_, p. 282).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: These are they which were not defiled with women; for they are virgins. These are they which follow the Lamb whithersoever he goeth. These were redeemed from among men, being the firstfruits unto God and to the Lamb.

pioneerReading.html: Smith reads **"women"** as a Rule-V symbol — corrupt churches: "**They were not defiled with women. A woman is in Scripture the symbol of a church, a virtuous woman representing a pure church, a corrupt woman an apostate church. It is, then, a characteristic of this company that at the time of their deliverance they are not defiled with, or have no connection with, the fallen churches of the land**" (DAR 584.4). On firstfruits: "**They are 'first-fruits' unto God and the Lamb... So the 144,000, ripening up for the heavenly garner here on earth during the troublous scenes of the last days, being translated to heaven without seeing death, and occupying a pre-eminent position, are, in this sense, as would seem very consistent, called first-fruits unto God and the Lamb**" (DAR 585.2).

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 13-14 (GC 648-649 verbatim) and pp. 371-378 (third-angel-message = righteousness by faith)
bohrReading.html: Bohr's verbatim quote of EGW (GC 649) inside the Rev 14:1-5 portrait: "_'These are they which follow the Lamb whithersoever He goeth.' [Revelation 14:1] These, having been translated from the earth, from among the living, are counted as 'the first fruits unto God and to the Lamb.' [Revelation 14:4]_" (_Close of Probation_, pp. 13-14, quoting GC 648-649). Bohr grounds the character-portrait of Rev 14:1-5 in the third-angel-message: "**Ellen White does not state that the third angel's message contains the message of righteousness by faith but rather it is righteousness by faith. Righteousness by faith is not one element of the third angel's message but rather permeates it all!**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 373, on Rev 14:9-12 producing the 14:1-5 character). On firstfruits Bohr leaves the substance to the harvest-of-the-earth reading at vv. 14-16.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent. Both Smith and Bohr read women = corrupt churches via Rule V (Rev 17 self-defines the Babylon harlot).

symbols.label: Symbols redefined
symbols.html: Women (both: corrupt churches, primarily Babylon and her daughters per Rev 17).

verses.text: And in their mouth was found no guile: for they are without fault before the throne of God.

pioneerReading.html: "**With this description of the 144,000 triumphant, the line of prophecy commencing with chapter 12 comes to a close**" (DAR 585.2). The without-fault language is taken under Smith's translation-without-seeing-death formulation at DAR 585.2: the 144,000 are translated to heaven from among the living, occupying a pre-eminent position as first-fruits unto God and the Lamb.

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 13-14, quoting GC 648-649 verbatim
bohrReading.html: Bohr quotes EGW (GC 649) verbatim with v.5 woven in: "_'In their mouth was found no guile: for they are without fault' [Revelation 14:5] before God_" (_Close of Probation_, pp. 13-14, quoting GC 648-649). Bohr ties this to the perfected character produced by the third angel's message: "**Righteousness by faith is not one element of the third angel's message but rather permeates it all!**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 373).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And I saw another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach unto them that dwell on the earth, and to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people,

pioneerReading.citation: DAR 585.3-621.2 (Smith's three-angel block; v.6 specifically documented at DAR 591.1-591.2 — the Advent movement that began with Miller in 1831 and culminated 1840-1844)
pioneerReading.html: Smith identifies the angel as a symbol of a **religious movement** preaching the everlasting gospel just prior to the Second Advent. The movement is the Millerite advent awakening: "**But the strongest and most conclusive evidence that the message belongs to the present time will consist in finding some movement in this generation through which its fulfillment has been, or is, going forward. On this point we refer to a movement of which it would now be hard to find any one who is wholly ignorant. It is the great Advent movement of the present century. As early as 1831, Wm. Miller, of Low Hampton, N. Y., by an earnest and consistent study of the prophecies, was led to the conclusion that the gospel dispensation was near its close. He placed the termination, which he thought would occur at the end of the prophetic periods, about the year 1843. This date was afterward extended to the autumn of 1844**" (DAR 591.1). Smith then runs the parallel European documentation (Wolff, Bengel, Gaussen, Mason, etc.) through DAR 592-620 and locks the message's fulfillment to 1840-1844.

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 66 (triune Godhead sending three angels); _Revelation's Seven Trumpets_, pp. 146-147 (1798 starting bracket), 227-228 (GC 356 quote), 246 (global extension), 263 (three common denominators with Rev 10)
bohrReading.html: Bohr extends the first-angel proclamation back to **1798** (the unsealing of Daniel under Rev 10) and frames the three angels as sent by the **triune Godhead**: "**In Revelation 14:6-13, the fourth and fifth trumpets have faded from view... In other words, Revelation 14:6-13 takes us back to the time when the remnant began to proclaim first angel's message to the world (beginning in 1798) after the fifth trumpet and first woe ended in 1798**" (_Seven Trumpets_, pp. 146-147). On the triune-Godhead framing: "**In Revelation the Godhead is described as triune (see Revelation 1:4, 5). At the end of time, the triune Godhead sends three angels (Revelation 14:6-12) to gather people from every nation, kindred, tongue and people on God's side**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 66). On the global scope: "**The Angel presents a message from the book that is global in extension—to every nation, kindred, tongue and people (Revelation 14:6)**" (_Seven Trumpets_, p. 246). On the Rev 10 / Rev 14 link: Bohr identifies **three common denominators** between Rev 10 and Rev 14:6, 7 — global message, Creator and Sabbath, judgment hour (_Seven Trumpets_, p. 263). Bohr quotes EGW (GC 355-356): "_this message [Revelation 14:6, 7] is a part of the gospel which could be proclaimed only in the last days, for only then would it be true that the hour of judgment had come_" (_Seven Trumpets_, pp. 227-228). **Diverges from Smith on chronological starting point**: Smith locks the angel to 1840-1844 (Millerite phase only); Bohr opens the window at 1798 (book-unsealed phase) and continues through 1844 and beyond. (Note: Bohr is internally inconsistent on the start date — at _Seven Trumpets_ p. 358 he writes the message began "in the 1830's and early 1840's," which is closer to Smith's bracket.)

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Both readings respect Rule IV (history confirms prophecy). Bohr's 1798 start is a Rule-V extension grounded in Rev 10:11 ("thou must prophesy again"), which Smith himself acknowledges as the same movement under a different chapter; the disagreement is on the **window's starting bracket**, not on its substance.

verses.text: Saying with a loud voice, Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come: and worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters.

pioneerReading.citation: DAR 588.2-621.2 (Smith's full chronological argument for the 1844 hour of judgment); seal-of-God / Sabbath-as-Creator's-memorial argument deployed earlier at DAR 439.5-440
pioneerReading.html: Smith reads "**the hour of his judgment is come**" as the **investigative judgment beginning in 1844** at the close of the 2300-day prophecy. The full chronological argument runs from DAR 588-621 (the bulk of the entire chapter). On the worship-the-Creator call as the Sabbath call, Smith's foundational argument is deployed at DAR 439.5-440 (in Rev 7 chapter, but applied throughout the Rev 14 block): "**we see at once that we are reading the requirements of Him who created all things. The sun, then, is not the God of the decalogue; the true God is he who made the sun. No object in heaven or earth is the being who here demands obedience; for the God of this law is the one who made all created things**" (DAR 439.5-440) — the Sabbath commandment with its "For in six days the Lord made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that in them is" is what identifies the true God of Rev 14:7.

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 66; _Revelation's Seven Trumpets_, p. 263 (three common denominators between Rev 10 and Rev 14:6-7: global message, Creator and Sabbath, judgment hour); _Studies on Daniel 11_, pp. 168-169 (worship-the-Creator vs worship-the-beast/king-of-the-north)
bohrReading.html: Bohr reads "hour of judgment" as the 1844 investigative judgment and the worship-the-Creator call as a Sabbath call. From his Rev 10 / Rev 14:6-7 comparison: "**There are three common denominators between Revelation 10 and Revelation 14:6, 7: Both contain a global message to every nation, kindred, tongue and people; Both draw attention to the Creator and His Sabbath; The little book contains the judgment hour message as does the first angel's message**" (_Seven Trumpets_, p. 263). On the binary worship-choice of Rev 14:7 vs Rev 14:9-11: "**The three angels' messages from heaven announce tidings or a warning to worship the Creator rather than the beast (the king of the north)**" (_Studies on Daniel 11_, p. 168-169). The hour-of-judgment / Creator / Sabbath chain matches Smith's Rule-V argument exactly.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Exemplary Rule-IV and Rule-V convergence (Lev 16 / Dan 7-8 / Exod 20:11 chain).

verses.text: And there followed another angel, saying, Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city, because she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication.

pioneerReading.citation: DAR 601.3-614.4 (Smith's second-message section: Babylon definition at 601.3; reason-for-fall at 611.1-611.2; 1844 fulfillment at 612.1-614.4)
pioneerReading.html: Smith reads the second angel as the **1844 cry** by the Millerite movement (with future Loud-Cry repeat at Rev 18:1-4 acknowledged but secondary). On Babylon = the apostate churches: "**Babylon is not confined to the Romish Church. That this church is a very prominent component part of great Babylon, is not denied... But the name which she bears on her forehead, 'Mystery, Babylon the Great, the Mother of Harlots and Abominations of the Earth,' reveals other family connections. If this church is the mother, who are the daughters? The fact that these daughters are spoken of, shows that there are other religious bodies besides the Romish Church which come under this designation**" (DAR 601.3). On the 1844 fulfillment: "**To come now more particularly to the application of the prophecy concerning the fall of Babylon, let us see how the religious world stood with reference to the possibility of such a change, when the time came for the proclamation of this message, in connection with the first message, about the year**" 1844 (DAR 610.3). The result on rejecting churches: "**A marked change then came over the churches in respect to their spiritual condition**" (DAR 612.2).

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 123-126 (Rev 14:8 + Rev 18:1-5 comparison; GC 603-604 quoted verbatim at p. 124)
bohrReading.html: Bohr explicitly stages v. 8 as a **two-stage** message: "**There is a close link between the second angel's message in Revelation 14:8 and the Loud Cry message of the Angel in Revelation 18:1-5. The Millerites proclaimed the message of Revelation 14:8 leading up to 1844. At that time, God's faithful children left the fallen Protestant Churches and joined those who were proclaiming the judgment hour message. Shortly before the close of probation the second angel's message will be proclaimed once more but with global extension and with far greater power**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 124). Bohr then quotes EGW (GC 603-604) verbatim: "_This scripture [Revelation 18:1-5] points forward to a time when the announcement of the fall of Babylon, as made by the second angel of Revelation 14 (verse 8), is to be repeated, with the additional mention of the corruptions which have been entering the various organizations that constitute Babylon, since that message was first given, in the summer of 1844_" (_Close of Probation_, p. 124). Bohr's comparison table (pp. 125-126) maps Rev 14:8 onto Rev 18:1-5 point for point. **Convergent with extension**: Smith acknowledges the Loud-Cry repetition but treats the 1844 cry as primary; Bohr treats both as one composite message in two stages.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Both readings respect Rule IV (history) and Rule V (Rev 18:1-5 as the prophetic parallel). The difference is one of emphasis.

symbols.label: Symbols redefined
symbols.html: Babylon (both: apostate religious world — Rome plus apostate Protestantism — per Rev 17).

verses.text: And the third angel followed them, saying with a loud voice, If any man worship the beast and his image, and receive his mark in his forehead, or in his hand,

pioneerReading.citation: DAR 624.1-624.6 (Smith's numbered identification of beast/image/mark from the third-angel section)
pioneerReading.html: Smith reads the third angel as the **Sabbath/Sunday warning** against the papal beast and the Protestant-American image. Numbered identifications: "**1. The 'beast' is the Roman Catholic power. 2. The 'mark of the beast' is that institution which this power has set up as proof of its authority to legislate for the church, and command the consciences of men under sin. It consists in a change of the law of God, by which the signature of royalty is taken from the law, — the seventh-day Sabbath... 3. The 'image of the beast' is some ecclesiastical combination, which will resemble the beast in being clothed with power to enforce its decrees with the pains and penalties of the civil law. 4. The two-horned beast, by which the image, after being made by the people, is given power to speak and act, is the United States... 5. The two-horned beast enforces the mark of the beast; that is, he establishes by law the observance of the first day of the week, or Sunday Sabbath**" (DAR 624.2-624.6).

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 371-378 (Lesson on the third angel = righteousness by faith)
bohrReading.html: Bohr reads the third angel as the warning against the Rev 13 beast (papacy) and its image (apostate Protestant America in coalition with Rome). Verbatim on the message's character: "**Ellen White does not state that the third angel's message contains the message of righteousness by faith but rather it is righteousness by faith. Righteousness by faith is not one element of the third angel's message but rather permeates it all!**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 373). The mark is Sunday observance enforced by the image. **Convergent on every substantive identification** with Smith.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Exemplary Rule-IV / Rule-V convergence.

verses.text: The same shall drink of the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture into the cup of his indignation; and he shall be tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb:

pioneerReading.citation: DAR 631.7-632.1 (third-angel punishment block); cross-link at DAR 506.3 (wrath = seven last plagues)
pioneerReading.html: Smith reads "wine of the wrath of God without mixture" as the seven last plagues of Rev 15-16. From the Punishment-of-Beast-worshipers section: "**These shall be tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels and of the Lamb. When is this torment inflicted? Chapter 19:20 shows that at the second coming of Christ there is a manifestation of fiery judgments which may be called a lake of fire and brimstone, into which the beast and false prophet are cast alive. This can refer only to the destruction visited upon them at the commencement, not at the end, of the thousand years**" (DAR 631.7). The wrath = seven plagues identification is at DAR 506.3: "**The wrath of God for the present generation is filled up in the seven last plagues (chapter 15:1), which consequently must here be referred to**". The fire-and-brimstone torment is the executive judgment.

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 5-7 (wine-without-mixture = seven plagues); _Revelation's Seven Trumpets_, pp. 20-21 (altar-fire angel ties Rev 14:18 / Rev 8:5)
bohrReading.html: Bohr reads v. 10 against the **seven plagues** backdrop. Explicit chain: "**the seven eschatological plagues are the full and total outpouring of his justice without mixture of mercy. Revelation 14:10 refers to this as 'the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture ["full strength, NKJV"] into the cup of his indignation'**" (_Close of Probation_, pp. 5-7). Bohr ties this to the altar-fire angel of Rev 14:18 / Rev 8:5 / Rev 15-16: "**[parallel to] the angel of fire who pours out God's wrath into the winepress of His fury (Revelation 14:18)**" (_Seven Trumpets_, p. 20). The wine-without-mixture chain runs Rev 14:10 → Rev 15:1 → Rev 16:1-2 → Rev 19:11ff. **Convergent with Smith on the wrath-without-mixture = seven plagues chain.**

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent; Rule V chain (Rev 14 → 15 → 16 → 19).

verses.text: And the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever: and they have no rest day nor night, who worship the beast and his image, and whosoever receiveth the mark of his name.

pioneerReading.citation: DAR 631.7-632.1 (Punishment / Duration sections of Smith's third-message exposition)
pioneerReading.html: Smith handles the "for ever and ever" smoke as the **final and complete destruction** (annihilation, not eternal conscious torment), citing Isa 34 (Edom's smoke ascending forever) as the Rule-V parallel. Verbatim: "**Duration of the Punishment. — The expression 'forever and ever' cannot here denote eternity. This is evident from the fact that this punishment is inflicted on this earth, where time is measured by day and night... in either case the scene must eventually terminate; for this earth is finally to be made new, cleansed of every stain of sin, every vestige of suffering and decay, and to become the habitation of righteousness and joy throughout eternal ages. The word αἰών here translated forever, Schrevelius, in his Greek Lexicon, defines thus: 'An age; a long period of time; indefinite duration; time, whether longer or shorter'**" (DAR 632.1). Smith reads the smoke ascending "for ever and ever" as describing the **utter and irreversible** character of the destruction (Isa 34:8-10 quoted at DAR 631.7), not endless duration of conscious suffering.

bohrReading.html: (Bohr does not give v. 11 a separate verse-by-verse treatment; the substance is covered under the three-angels block at _Close of Probation_, p. 66, and the executive-judgment block at pp. 372-378, with annihilationism implicit in his treatment of the lake of fire in the New Earth volume.)

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No discrete Bohr exegesis to score; Smith's annihilationist reading is the SDA-standard Rule-V (Isa 34 / Jude 7 / Mal 4:1-3 / 2 Pet 2:6) reading.

verses.text: Here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus.

pioneerReading.html: Smith reads the verse as the **distinguishing badge** of the third-angel people: commandment-keeping (specifically the fourth commandment, against the mark) and the faith of Jesus. Verbatim: "**The period of the third message is a time of patience with the people of God. Paul and James both give us instruction on this point. Hebrews 10:36; James 5:7, 8. Meanwhile, this waiting company are keeping the commandments of God — the ten commandments, and the faith of Jesus — all the teachings of Christ and his apostles as contained in the New Testament. The true Sabbath, as given in the decalogue, is thus brought out in vivid contrast with the counterfeit Sabbath, the mark of the beast, which finally distinguishes those who reject the third message, as already set forth**" (DAR 633.1).

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 371-378 (Lesson on the third angel = righteousness by faith)
bohrReading.html: Bohr reads v. 12 as the third-angel saints — characterized by commandment-keeping plus the faith of Jesus (righteousness by faith): "**There are three key words in Revelation 14:12 that identify the faithful remnant: they have the patience of the saints, they keep the commandments of God and have the faith of Jesus. These three concepts must be understood in the context of verses 9-11**" (_Close of Probation_, pp. 373-374). "**Righteousness by faith is not one element of the third angel's message but rather permeates it all!**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 373). Bohr ties the v. 12 character profile to the Rev 14:1-5 character portrait. **Convergent with Smith.**

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And I heard a voice from heaven saying unto me, Write, Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord from henceforth: Yea, saith the Spirit, that they may rest from their labours; and their works do follow them.

pioneerReading.citation: DAR 634.1 (full v.13 exposition including the special-resurrection treatment woven into the body, not a footnote)
pioneerReading.html: Smith reads "**from henceforth**" as **from the rise of the third angel's message** (1844 onward). Verbatim: "**A Blessing Promised. — John is commanded by a voice from heaven to write, 'Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord from henceforth'... 'From henceforth' must signify from some particular point of time. What point? — Evidently from the commencement of the message in connection with which this is spoken. But why are those who die after this point of time blessed?... they have an advantage over them in being, doubtless, that company spoken of in Daniel 12:2, who are raised to everlasting life at the standing up of Michael... Those who die after having become identified with the third angel's message, are evidently numbered as a part of the 144,000... Being raised in the special resurrection (Daniel 12:2; Revelation 1:7) which occurs when the voice of God is uttered from the temple, at the beginning of the seventh and last plague (Revelation 16:17; Joel 3:16; Hebrews 12:26), they pass through the period of that plague, and hence may be said to come 'out of great tribulation' (Revelation 7:14)**" (DAR 634.1).

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 221-228 (Lesson #12 — The Special Resurrection)
bohrReading.html: Bohr makes the special resurrection the centerpiece of his v. 13 treatment, anchoring it to Dan 12:2: "**Therefore, God pronounces a special blessing upon those who die in the faith of the third angel's message from 1844 on**" (p. 222). The lesson opens explicitly at Rev 14:13: "**In the previous quote, Ellen White cites Revelation 14:13 as the biblical evidence that some will go to their rest before the time of trouble. It is noteworthy that the blessing that God pronounces upon those who sleep comes immediately after the third angel's message**" (p. 222). Bohr links Rev 14:13 → Dan 12:2 → Rev 1:7 in the same chain Smith uses. **Convergent on substance**; Bohr expands what Smith places inside the v. 13 paragraph into a stand-alone 6-7 page lesson chapter.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Exemplary Rule-V convergence (Rev 14:13 + Dan 12:2 + Rev 1:7).

verses.text: And I looked, and behold a white cloud, and upon the cloud one sat like unto the Son of man, having on his head a golden crown, and in his hand a sharp sickle.

pioneerReading.html: Smith identifies the Son of man on the cloud as **Christ at the close of his priestly mediation, about to come for the harvest** (still seated in the heavenly sanctuary, sickle in hand, but before he appears in the clouds of heaven): "**It will be noticed that in this line of prophecy, three angels precede the Son of man on the white cloud, and three are introduced after that symbol... The message of the fourth angel is evidently to be uttered after the Son of man, having finished his priestly work, takes his seat upon the white cloud, but before he appears in the clouds of heaven**" (DAR 635.1). The harvest itself (vv. 15-16) is the **ingathering of the righteous** at the Second Coming: "**By this language we are carried down past the second Advent, with its accompanying scenes of destruction to the wicked and salvation to the righteous**" (DAR 635.2).

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 69, 161, 194
bohrReading.html: Bohr identifies the Son of man on the cloud as Christ at the harvest of the righteous, with the three angels' messages having ripened the harvest. Verbatim: "**Revelation 14:14-17: When the harvest of the earth is ripe, Jesus sits on a cloud and reaps it**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 161). On the literary structure: "**Revelation 14:14-17 globalizes Joel 3:9-12**" and "**Revelation 14:14-18: The message of the three angels ripens the harvest and the grapes for the harvest**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 69). "**Jesus harvests the righteous and they are gathered in spiritual Jerusalem (14:15, 16; see Joel 2:32; 3:9-12)**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 194). **Convergent with Smith on Son-of-man = Christ at the harvest of the righteous.**

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And another angel came out of the temple, crying with a loud voice to him that sat on the cloud, Thrust in thy sickle, and reap: for the time is come for thee to reap; for the harvest of the earth is ripe.

pioneerReading.html: Smith reads the angel from the temple — addressing the Son of man already seated upon the cloud — as **the church's day-and-night prayer** (Luke 18:7-8) after probation has closed, asking Christ to come: "**As the language is addressed to Him who is seated upon the white cloud, having in his hand a sharp sickle ready to reap, it must denote a message of prayer on the part of the church, after their work for the world is done and probation has ceased, and nothing remains but for the Lord to appear and take his people to himself. It is doubtless the day-and-night cry spoken of by our Lord in Luke 18:7, 8 in connection with the coming of the Son of man. And this prayer will be answered; the elect will be avenged**" (DAR 635.1).

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 194
bohrReading.html: Bohr treats v. 15 inside the broader harvest summary: "**Jesus harvests the righteous and they are gathered in spiritual Jerusalem (14:15, 16; see Joel 2:32; 3:9-12)**" (p. 194). He does not have a discrete commentary identifying the angel from the temple as the church's prayer (Smith's specific Luke 18 identification); for Bohr the angel's announcement marks the **close of probation** moment after which Christ reaps. **Convergent on harvest identification; Bohr is silent on Smith's church-prayer reading of the angel.**

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And he that sat on the cloud thrust in his sickle on the earth; and the earth was reaped.

pioneerReading.html: Smith reads v. 16 as **the ingathering of the saints at the Second Coming**: "**He that is seated upon the cloud will thrust in his sickle, and the saints, under the figure of the wheat of the earth, will be gathered into the heavenly garner**" (DAR 635.1). And on the actual reaping language: "**'And he that sat on the cloud,' says the prophecy, 'thrust in his sickle on the earth; and the earth was reaped.' By this language we are carried down past the second Advent, with its accompanying scenes of destruction to the wicked and salvation to the righteous**" (DAR 635.2, sub-heading: "The Wheat Garnered").

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 194
bohrReading.html: "**Jesus harvests the righteous and they are gathered in spiritual Jerusalem (14:15, 16; see Joel 2:32; 3:9-12)**" (p. 194). **Convergent with Smith on harvest = ingathering of the righteous at the Second Coming.**

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And another angel came out of the temple which is in heaven, he also having a sharp sickle.

pioneerReading.html: Smith reads the **second angel** (with a second sickle, vv. 17-18) as a new symbol introducing the **vintage** — distinct from the harvest of vv. 14-16, and aimed at the wicked: "**The Winepress of God's Wrath. — The last two angels have to do with the wicked, — the wicked, most fitly represented by the bloated and purple clusters of the vine of the earth**" (DAR 636.1).

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 161-162
bohrReading.html: Bohr reads this angel inside the literary flow Rev 14:14-17 (harvest of the righteous) → Rev 14:17-20 (vintage of the wicked). Verbatim: "**Revelation 14:14-17: When the harvest of the earth is ripe, Jesus sits on a cloud and reaps it**" (p. 161); then on the very next page, distinguishing the second sickle's referent: "**Revelation 14:17-20: When probation closes, God's spiritual Israel (the harvest) is gathered inside worldwide spiritual Mt. Zion and the wicked spiritual Babylonians (the grapes) are gathered in the worldwide spiritual winepress outside the city intent on destroying those who are inside (see Joel 3)**" (p. 162). **Convergent on the wicked-vintage identification.**

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And another angel came out from the altar, which had power over fire; and cried with a loud cry to him that had the sharp sickle, saying, Thrust in thy sickle, and gather the clusters of the vine of the earth; for her grapes are fully ripe.

pioneerReading.html: Smith reads the altar-angel-with-fire as the executive-judgment messenger directing the vintage of the wicked. The two last angels (v. 17 sickle-bearer + v. 18 altar-fire) jointly carry out the vintage: "**The Winepress of God's Wrath. — The last two angels have to do with the wicked, — the wicked, most fitly represented by the bloated and purple clusters of the vine of the earth. May it not be that the closing doom of that class at the end of the thousand years is here presented**" (DAR 636.1).

bohrReading.citation: _Revelation's Seven Trumpets_, pp. 20-21; _Close of Probation_, p. 194
bohrReading.html: Bohr explicitly ties the altar-fire angel to Ezek 10's man-in-linen scattering coals over the city — i.e., divine judgment from the altar: "**The man clothed in linen takes coals from between the cherubim and scatters them over the city as a sign of divine judgment. This is parallel to the angel of fire who pours out God's wrath into the winepress of His fury (Revelation 14:18)**" (_Seven Trumpets_, pp. 20-21). "**Jesus then harvests the wicked and throws them into the winepress outside spiritual Jerusalem (14:18, 19)**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 194). **Convergent on the altar-fire / executive-judgment chain.**

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And the angel thrust in his sickle into the earth, and gathered the vine of the earth, and cast it into the great winepress of the wrath of God.

pioneerReading.html: Smith reads the winepress as the **post-millennial executive judgment** — the destruction of the wicked AFTER the 1000 years, at the descent of the New Jerusalem (Rev 20:9). He argues this from the chapter sequence: the wicked are reserved for the closing doom that comes at the end of the 1000 years, NOT at the same moment as the harvest. Verbatim: "**The Winepress of God's Wrath. — The last two angels have to do with the wicked, — the wicked, most fitly represented by the bloated and purple clusters of the vine of the earth. May it not be that the closing doom of that class at the end of the thousand years is here presented, the prophecy thus making a final disposition of both the righteous and the wicked; the righteous clothed with immortality, and safely established in the kingdom, the wicked perishing around the city at the time of its ultimate location upon the earth?**" (DAR 636.1). And explicitly ruling out the Second Coming application: "**This can hardly be applied at the time of the second advent; for events are here given in chronological order; and the destruction of the wicked would be contemporaneous with the gathering of the righteous. Again, the living wicked at Christ's coming drink of the 'cup' of his indignation; but this passage brings to view the time when they perish in the 'winepress' of his wrath, which is said to be trodden 'without the city,' answering completely to the description of Revelation 20:9; and this latter expression would more naturally denote their complete and final destruction**" (DAR 636.2).

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 69, 162, 194; _Revelation's Seven Trumpets_, p. 358
bohrReading.html: Bohr locates the winepress at the **pre-second-coming / Armageddon-seventh-plague** moment — when probation closes and the wicked are gathered for destruction at Christ's return, with Rev 19:11ff as the parallel rather than Rev 20:9. Verbatim: "**Revelation 14:14-20: When the two groups are complete, the seventh trumpet is about to sound. The harvest of the earth and the grapes of the earth are ripe. The remnant is in spiritual Jerusalem while outside the city are the enemies who are intent on destroying the remnant (Revelation 14:18-20; see the background of Joel 3)**" (_Seven Trumpets_, p. 358). "**Revelation 14:17-20: When probation closes, God's spiritual Israel (the harvest) is gathered inside worldwide spiritual Mt. Zion and the wicked spiritual Babylonians (the grapes) are gathered in the worldwide spiritual winepress outside the city intent on destroying those who are inside (see Joel 3). At the critical moment, the horse riders trample the winepress and deliver those in the city**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 162). On the globalization of Joel 3 and the identity of the horse riders: "**Revelation 14:14-17 globalizes Joel 3:9-12... The horses that trample the winepress in Revelation 14:20 are later identified in Revelation 19:11ff as Jesus and the angelic hosts who come to trample the wicked in the winepress**" (_Close of Probation_, pp. 69-70). And: "**Jesus then harvests the wicked and throws them into the winepress outside spiritual Jerusalem (14:18, 19)**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 194). **Diverges from Smith on chronology**: Smith places the trampling **post-millennially** (Rev 20:9 cycle, explicitly denying a Second-Coming application); Bohr places it **pre-second-coming / Armageddon** (the seventh plague / Rev 19:11ff at Christ's return, before the 1000 years).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: **Bohr is marginal on Rule V (scripture-its-own-expositor — which intra-Revelation parallel governs) and Rule XI (literal/figurative — the identity of 'the city'), and the two readings are chronologically incompatible.** Smith's post-millennial reading is anchored by the explicit Rule-V chain: Rev 14:20's "without the city" + "winepress" matches Rev 20:9 ("the camp of the saints, and the beloved city") and Smith makes this match decisive (DAR 636.2). He rejects the Second-Coming application on Rule-V grounds because at the Second Coming the wicked drink the "cup" (v. 10) of indignation, not the "winepress" of his wrath (v. 19) — different symbol, different moment. Bohr's pre-coming reading anchors instead to Rev 19:11ff (the King of kings + armies of heaven treading the winepress) and treats that as the controlling parallel, collapsing the Rev 19 / Rev 20:9 distinction Smith insists on. Both apply Rule V; they disagree on **which intra-Revelation parallel is decisive**. EGW (GC 657-672) tends to read the winepress as **both** the second-coming destruction AND the post-millennial executive judgment (composite), which softens but does not fully resolve the divergence. **This is the single most significant chronological recompose Bohr applies to Rev 14**, and Smith's explicit rejection of the second-advent application (DAR 636.2) means Bohr is unambiguously on the other side of an issue Smith treated as Rule-V settled.

symbols.label: Symbols redefined
symbols.html: Winepress / vintage chronology (Smith: post-millennial — Rev 20:9, with the second-advent application explicitly rejected; Bohr: pre-coming / Armageddon — Rev 19:11ff at the Second Coming, with the seventh-plague backdrop).

verses.text: And the winepress was trodden without the city, and blood came out of the winepress, even unto the horse bridles, by the space of a thousand and six hundred furlongs.

pioneerReading.html: Smith reads the **"without the city"** as outside the New Jerusalem after its post-millennial descent, via the explicit Rev 20:9 match. He explicitly rejects a Second-Coming application: "**This can hardly be applied at the time of the second advent; for events are here given in chronological order; and the destruction of the wicked would be contemporaneous with the gathering of the righteous. Again, the living wicked at Christ's coming drink of the 'cup' of his indignation; but this passage brings to view the time when they perish in the 'winepress' of his wrath, which is said to be trodden 'without the city,' answering completely to the description of Revelation 20:9; and this latter expression would more naturally denote their complete and final destruction**" (DAR 636.2). Smith does not pause on the 1600-furlong measure as a literal datum; the post-millennial framing controls the verse.

bohrReading.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 114-115, 194
bohrReading.html: Bohr reads "without the city" as outside **spiritual Mt Zion** (the worldwide gathering of the remnant) — i.e., outside the spiritual city of God's people who are gathered inside at the Second Coming. Verbatim on the inside/outside distinction: "**In Revelation 16:16 the kings of the earth and the whole world are gathered in the place whose name means 'Mount of the Congregation'. Their intention in gathering there is to slay God's people who are spiritually on Mt. Zion. God's people are depicted as the harvest of the earth inside the city and the wicked are portrayed as the grapes gathered outside the city (Revelation 14:20)**" (pp. 114-115). And on the harvest summary: "**Horses trample the winepress and blood splatters on the horses (14:20)**" (p. 194). **Diverges from Smith on the city's identity and on chronology**: Smith reads "the city" as the New Jerusalem post-descent (Rev 20:9 / Rev 21:2); Bohr reads it as spiritual Mt Zion (the worldwide remnant gathered at the Second Coming, before the 1000 years).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: **Same Rule-V / Rule-XI marginal-violation issue as v. 19** — Bohr's spiritual-Mt-Zion-at-the-Second-Coming reading is the front end of his pre-millennial winepress chronology. The Rev 19:11ff parallel (where the King of kings rides with the armies of heaven and treads the winepress at the Second Coming) supports Bohr; Smith's Rev 20:9 match ("camp of the saints / beloved city" + "without the city") supports Smith and is the one Smith treats as decisive. The 1600-furlong measure neither reading treats with strict literalness; both read it symbolically (universality of the slaughter / 4 × 4 × 100 completeness number).

symbols.label: Symbols redefined
symbols.html: The city (Smith: New Jerusalem post-descent — Rev 20:9 / Rev 21:2; Bohr: spiritual Mt Zion at the Second Coming — Rev 14:1's worldwide remnant gathering).

summaryHtml:
**Rev 14 violation summary (against _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, _Revelation's Seven Trumpets_, _Revelation's Seven Seals_, and _Studies on Daniel 11_):** Of 20 verses, Bohr has Rule-V / Rule-XI marginal violations on **2** (vv. 19, 20 — the post-millennial-vs-pre-coming winepress chronology, the single most significant chronological recompose in Bohr's Rev 14 treatment, and the one place Smith explicitly rejects the Second-Coming application that Bohr embraces — DAR 636.2). The chapter's core agrees with pioneer on every major identification: Mt Zion = the redeemed; 144,000 = the last-generation translated saints; three angels = three successive end-time messages (first = 1844 hour-of-judgment / worship-the-Creator-Sabbath; second = Babylon fallen, 1844 + future Loud Cry; third = mark-of-beast / Sabbath-Sunday warning); v. 13 special blessing from 1844 (with Smith-footnote special resurrection that Bohr expands into a 6-page treatment); Son of man on the cloud = Christ at the harvest of the righteous; harvest of vv. 14-16 = righteous ingathered; vintage of vv. 17-20 = wicked destroyed. Bohr's distinctive extensions are: (a) the **four-portraits framework** of the 144,000 (Rev 7 + 14 + 15 + 19 read as one composite — a Rule-V harmonization, not a violation); (b) the **1798 starting bracket** for the first angel (Rev 10 unsealing → Rev 14:6 proclamation — defensible Rule-V chain, though Bohr is internally inconsistent: _Seven Trumpets_ p. 147 says 1798, p. 358 says "1830's and early 1840's"); (c) the **two-stage second angel** (1844 + future Loud Cry — convergent with EGW on Rev 18:1 swelling cry); (d) the **spiritual-Mt-Zion-as-worldwide-remnant** reading where Smith leaves Mt Zion as the heavenly locale (marginal — see vv. 19, 20). On v. 13 Bohr is _more_ developed than Smith (the 6-page special-resurrection treatment expands what Smith handles in a footnote). Verses where Bohr offers no discrete verse-by-verse exegesis but the substance is covered under larger blocks: v. 11 (annihilationist torment) covered implicitly under the third-angel's executive-judgment block.

---
